data.title: ESPN, ABC back on YouTube
data.description:
YouTube TV and Disney have reached a deal that will restore ESPN, ABC and other channels to the platform’s 10 million subscribers after nearly a two-week hiatus, the companies announced Friday night.

The deal will give Alphabet's (GOOG) (GOOGL)YouTube TV subscribers access to ESPN’s new direct-to-consumer “Unlimited” service at no additional cost, as part of YouTube TV’s base plan. That is most notable for WWE fans, as WWE’s major events — including Wrestlemania — are now part of ESPN’s Unlimited offering. The Unlimited service will also be accessible directly on the YouTube TV platform.

YouTube TV subscribers pay a little more than $80 per month for the base plan. The rollout will be completed by the end of 2026.

All of Disney’s channels will remain on YouTube TV’s service, led by ABC and all of ESPN’s networks.

In a statement, YouTube TV said, “We’re happy to share that we’ve reached an agreement with Disney that preserves the value of our service for our subscribers and future flexibility in our offers. Subscribers should see channels including ABC, ESPN and FX returning to their service over the course of the day, as well as any recordings that were previously in their Library. We apologize for the disruption and appreciate our subscribers’ patience as we negotiated on their behalf.”

Disney Entertainment co-chairmen Alan Bergman ad Dana Walden and ESPN chairman Jimmy Pitaro said, “This new agreement reflects our continued commitment to delivering exceptional entertainment and evolving with how audiences choose to watch. It recognizes the tremendous value of Disney’s programming and provides YouTube TV subscribers with more flexibility and choice. We are pleased that our networks have been restored in time for fans to enjoy the many great programming options this weekend, including college football.”

Since Oct. 30th, encompassing two weekends and Monday nights, YouTube TV subscribers have gone without ESPN’s expansive college football slate of games and “Monday Night Football.”

data.title: Beam Global outlines Middle East expansion and recurring revenue strategy amid Q3 order delays
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Beam Global (BEEM) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Desmond Wheatley highlighted the launch of "Beam Middle East," a 50-50 joint venture with the Platinum Group, stating the structure ensures Beam is "paid back for any cost we incur before there's any profit share." Wheatley reported, "I've been having an exciting and very fruitful week here in Abu Dhabi, and I'm absolutely thrilled about what we've done in creating Beam Middle East."
  • Beam Middle East was featured at DRIFTx in Abu Dhabi, where Beam's EV ARC and BeamBike demonstrated unique, fully autonomous off-grid charging capabilities. Wheatley described the response as "unbelievable" with several companies engaged for potential collaboration.
  • Wheatley detailed new product focus areas, including the BeamFlight autonomous drone charging system, which enables drones to charge in the field, emphasizing its dual-use in military and commercial settings.
  • The company recorded a 21% increase in its energy storage systems business, with revenue driven primarily by new customers seeking specialized, high-density battery solutions.
  • CFO Lisa Potok reported revenues of $5.8 million for Q3 2025 and noted a shift in customer mix: "67% of those revenues were derived from commercial customers compared to only 31% in the same period of '24." International customers made up 39% of revenues in 2025, up from 20% in 2024. Potok added, "Our contracted backlog as of the end of Q3 of '25 was $8 million."
  • Potok described Q3 gross profit as a loss of $28,000, attributing it to "the impact of fixed overhead allocations on reduced reported sales." Excluding noncash items, non-GAAP gross margin for Q3 would be 13%.

Outlook

  • Management expects delayed orders, including an anticipated $3 million EV ARC order, to be executed in the first half of next year, which Wheatley said would have brought "revenues closer to $10 million with significantly improved gross margins of around 20% gap."
  • Wheatley stressed, "I think it's unlikely that it will happen in the fourth quarter this year, but I do think it's very likely that it will happen in the first half of next year when the new budgets are finalized."
  • Beam anticipates further growth from Beam Europe and new revenue streams from Beam Middle East, with Wheatley "very bullish about our opportunities here."
  • Recurring revenue models are in focus, particularly from BeamBike, as Wheatley said, "much of the revenue that we received from that initiative will, I believe, be recurring in the future."

Financial Results

  • Q3 2025 revenues were $5.8 million, with year-to-date revenues of $19.2 million compared to $40.9 million for the same period in 2024.
  • Gross margin loss for Q3 was 0.5%, versus an 11% gross margin for the prior year. Excluding noncash depreciation and amortization, Q3 gross margin would be 13%.
  • Operating expenses for Q3 were $4.8 million, with noncash impacts contributing to a total of $26.8 million. Without noncash items, operating expenses were $11.4 million, improved by $1.8 million year-over-year.
  • Net loss for Q3 was $4.9 million, compared to net profit of $1.3 million in the prior year. Excluding noncash items, Q3 net loss was $2.8 million, an improvement of $0.2 million over the prior year.
  • Cash balance at end of September was $3.3 million, with working capital of $10.9 million.

Q&A

  • Tate Sullivan, Maxim Group: Asked if wireless charging had been commercialized before and about recent market interest. Wheatley responded, "I've known for a long time that the wireless charging solution was going to be very important for the autonomous vehicle market...what's different now is...we now have a market to provide it to, and that's why you're going to see an awful lot more activity on it."
  • Sullivan: Asked about trial periods for battery customers. Wheatley explained, "some instances, we'll be able to move quite quickly to orders. In other instances, we'll have longer development periods, but we often get paid for that."
  • Ryan Pfingst, B. Riley Securities: Inquired about backlog composition and Middle East pipeline cadence. Wheatley noted, "while previously, backlog and pipeline was made up almost entirely of EV ARC sales...now we're looking at a situation where...none of our pipeline or backlog is made up of federal customers...most importantly, what you're seeing much more diversified."
  • Pfingst: Asked about timing for Middle East revenue. Wheatley said, "I think we'll be announcing stuff in the not-too-distant future."
  • Noel Parks, Tuohy Brothers: Asked about manufacturing capacity. Wheatley stated, "Our manufacturing capacity is underutilized at the moment because, of course, we've had this decline...but the answer to your question is we have excess capacity in the U.S. right now...and our acquisition in Europe got us 5x the factory space."
  • Parks: Asked about maintenance and repair trends for the EV ARC fleet. Wheatley confirmed, "We've seen costs associated with warranty expenditures. And again, those are all baked into our financials, but certainly not chronic."
  • [Dick Grill], private investor: Raised concerns about the stock price and dividends. Wheatley attributed price decline to "a massive rotation out of growth stocks," but added, "I believe that we will see new highs again in the future."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on commercialization pace, backlog mix, and revenue timing for new initiatives, reflecting a neutral to slightly negative tone amid order delays and revenue softness.
  • Management emphasized confidence in new market opportunities and product innovation, but Wheatley’s tone was measured, highlighting challenges in the U.S. market while maintaining optimism: "I'm very bullish about our opportunities here."
  • Compared to Q2, management’s tone was less energetic but remained constructive, with more focus on navigating delays and expanding into new markets.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q3 saw a drop in revenue from $7.1 million in Q2 to $5.8 million, attributed to delayed government orders.
  • Gross margin deteriorated from 20% in Q2 to a loss in Q3, but non-GAAP margins excluding noncash items remained positive.
  • Operating expenses and cash position remained stable, with improved efficiency highlighted by reduced expenses.
  • Management’s narrative shifted from Q2’s focus on international expansion and margin improvement to addressing delayed orders and broadening product and geographic diversification.
  • Analyst focus shifted from growth and margin improvement in Q2 to order delays, recurring revenue, and diversification in Q3.

Risks and Concerns

  • Revenue declines attributed to order timing and political factors in the U.S. and Europe.
  • Delays in government funding affected major orders, specifically a $3 million EV ARC order.
  • Political unrest in Serbia and the Balkans impacted European operations.
  • Warranty and maintenance costs for aging EV ARC units were mentioned but described as manageable.
  • Management cited ongoing cost discipline and efficiency improvements to mitigate risks from market softness and delayed orders.

Final Takeaway

Beam Global management underscored a challenging quarter marked by delayed government orders and revenue declines, but highlighted the company’s strategic pivot toward new markets and recurring revenue streams. The launch of Beam Middle East, expanded product offerings like BeamBike and BeamFlight, and a focus on cost discipline position the company to capitalize on global electrification trends and diversify away from U.S. federal dependence. Management remains optimistic about upcoming order conversions and future growth as new initiatives take hold.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: International Paper to shut two packaging plants on weak demand
data.description:
International Paper (IP) said Friday it will close packaging plants in Compton, California, and Louisville, Kentucky, as the company cuts costs and consolidates operations in a weak demand environment.

The facilities will cease operations by January 2026, impacting 218 employees; the company said production will shift to nearby sites.

"The company will work to minimize the impact on employees by using attrition, retirements and current vacancies at other International Paper locations," Executive VP Tom Hamic said.

International Paper (IP), which last month reported a wider than expected Q3 adjusted loss, has shut underperforming plants and raised prices as the world's largest packaging company by revenue copes with soft demand and higher costs, including tariffs.

data.title: TOMI outlines expanded SteraMist pipeline and expects $3M integration contracts to close by year-end as recurring revenue rises
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: TOMI Environmental Solutions (TOMZ) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Halden Shane stated the third quarter was a “decisive turning point,” highlighting a 95% sequential revenue increase and emphasizing the expansion of recurring revenue streams and deeper customer engagement. He attributed momentum to increased capital purchases of mobile equipment by both industry leaders and smaller partners, with the SteraMist iHP technology gaining broader traction.
  • Shane reported, “Our third quarter revenue reached $2 million marking a 95% increase over quarter 2. This growth was driven by strong equipment sales and most notably, recurring BIT Solution sales which have risen 21% year-to-date.”
  • He stressed the importance of a growing sales order backlog, noting it was $0.9 million at quarter end and had grown to $1.3 million by October 31, with approximately $3 million in pending SteraMist Integration Contract expected to close before year-end. The active pipeline surpassed $15 million, spanning domestic and international customers.
  • Shane described broadening regulatory approvals and industry recognition, referencing the FDA’s expanded approval of hydrogen peroxide and SteraMist’s efficacy against honeybee colony collapse as opening new markets in agriculture, food processing, and biosafety.
  • “On November 5, we entered into an equity purchase agreement with Hudson Global Ventures, giving us the flexible right but not the obligation to sell up to $20 million in common stock over a 24-month period,” Shane said, noting the move provides flexible capital for development and expansion.
  • CFO David Vanston reported, “In the third quarter of 2025, our revenue was $2 million, a decline from $2.5 million in Q3 '24, representing a 24% decrease in sales. This was primarily driven by a timing reduction in the quarter of iHP services sales from a key customer who reorganized its operations. This impact is expected to be temporary, and the customers' operations and related service activity are expected to resume to a normal level in the near future.”
  • COO Elissa Shane explained, “We are currently negotiating an additional $3 million in custom and integrated contracts with this expected to close before the year-end,” and highlighted new deployments, repeat orders from global leaders, and an expanded customer base in pharma, healthcare, and academic sectors.

Outlook

  • Management cited a strong active pipeline above $15 million, with $3 million in pending integration contracts expected to close before year-end. Shane said, “We are motivated by the progress in our strategic partnerships and the growing interest from clients seeking to improve their operations with our solutions.”
  • Strategic focus is on scaling automation integrations, expanding into sustainable agriculture, and developing collaborative R&D.
  • The company expects recurring solution sales to “pop up again” by year-end and intends to leverage equipment sales to drive further growth in solution sales.

Financial Results

  • Revenue for Q3 2025 was $2 million, down from $2.5 million in Q3 2024. Year-to-date solution revenue was $760,000, representing a 21% increase year-on-year.
  • Gross profit margins remained strong at 61% for Q3 2025, consistent with the same period last year.
  • The company experienced an operating loss of approximately $320,000 in Q3 2025 versus operating income of $149,000 in Q3 2024. Net loss for Q3 2025 was approximately $450,000 or ($0.02) per share, compared to net income of $58,000 in the prior-year quarter.
  • Cash and cash equivalents stood at about $190,000, with working capital of $2.5 million and shareholders' equity of $2.2 million as of September 30, 2025.

Q&A

  • Sameer Joshi, H.C. Wainwright: Asked about FDA approval impact and targeted customers. Chairman Shane responded that the approval is new and presents game-changing opportunities in food processing, storage, packaging, and transportation, but “it’s a little too early” to detail specific customers as many are not yet aware of the change.
  • Joshi asked about the sustainability of operating expenses and gross margins. Shane said expenses will rise with scaling, but “they’re going to be positive in relationship to revenue.” Vanston added, “I don't expect our margin to be under too much pressure.”
  • Joshi inquired about international sales efforts. Shane said, “We have different distributors in some of these countries that are focused in them,” and cited global partners implementing SteraMist internationally.
  • Joshi questioned visibility for future solution sales. Shane noted recurring sales should increase by year-end as more equipment is deployed, driving solution sales growth.
  • John Nelson, private investor: Asked about military and defense markets. COO Elissa Shane said, “They are looking to replace formaldehyde and have been, and we're working with them,” expecting a significant development in 2026.
  • Nelson asked about healthcare mold remediation provider rollout. COO Shane detailed rapid adoption by new providers and ongoing training, expecting expanded reach and case studies.
  • Nelson raised opportunities in the data center market. Shane said the company is interested but needs to increase its sales force to pursue these large opportunities.
  • Additional questions addressed marketing strategies for FDA-approved applications and efforts to replace ethylene oxide in medical sterilization, with COO Shane confirming these as current priorities.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were positive, congratulating management on the “nice turnaround” and enhanced momentum, but pressed for details on cost control, margin sustainability, and customer targeting. Inquiries reflected optimism about market expansion and recurring revenue but sought clarity on execution and timing.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing “momentum,” “growth,” and “game-changing” opportunities, but were cautious about providing specific sales forecasts, noting that it was “too early” to quantify certain market impacts.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management displayed higher optimism, with management expressing increased confidence in the business model’s scalability and recurring revenue potential.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Sequential revenue increased 95% from Q2 2025 to Q3 2025, reversing the prior quarter’s decline. Solution sales, though slowing from earlier in the year, maintained a 21% year-to-date increase.
  • Backlog and pipeline expanded, with $1.3 million in sales order backlog by October 31 and $3 million in pending integration contracts, up from $2 million in the previous quarter.
  • Management’s tone shifted from cautious optimism to more assertive confidence, citing tangible signs of recovery and growth.
  • Analysts moved from concern over deferred CapEx and regulatory hurdles in Q2 to a stronger focus on execution and market penetration in Q3.

Risks and Concerns

  • CFO Vanston cited a “timing reduction” in iHP service sales from a key customer due to operational reorganization, but expects activity to resume.
  • Management acknowledged the need to expand the sales force and technical team to address larger opportunities, particularly in new verticals such as data centers.
  • The company’s cash position decreased from $569,000 in Q2 to $190,000 in Q3, highlighting ongoing liquidity management as a concern.
  • Extended timelines for Custom Engineered System projects were acknowledged, with a strategic shift toward hybrid and SIS products for faster revenue realization.

Final Takeaway

TOMI Environmental Solutions reported a sharp sequential rebound in revenue for Q3 2025, driven by recurring solution sales, equipment adoption, and an expanding pipeline of integration contracts. The company is leveraging new FDA approvals, regulatory tailwinds, and industry partnerships to pursue opportunities across food safety, pharmaceuticals, and biosafety. Management highlighted a significant backlog, a robust active pipeline, and the flexibility to raise up to $20 million in equity to support growth initiatives. While the company faces operating losses and a lower cash balance, its strategic focus on scaling through automation, expanding into new sectors, and strengthening its sales force underpins an optimistic outlook for the remainder of 2025 and into 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Quantum Computing Inc. outlines path to volume production and expands strategic partnerships amid $1.5B capital raise
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Quantum Computing Inc. (QUBT) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Yuping Huang, CEO, President, Chief Quantum Officer & Chairman, described the third quarter as a pivotal period marked by a strengthened balance sheet, a growing portfolio of commercial relationships, and a disciplined scaling strategy. He stated, "To date, in 2025, we have raised over $1.5 billion in capital, and we now have the resources to execute thoughtfully on our long-term vision, putting quantum technology into the hands of people."
  • The company raised $500 million in the third quarter and subsequently $750 million, positioning itself for investments in engineering, manufacturing, and sales. Huang emphasized, "Our long-term goal is to move from prototype and small batch manufacturing towards volume production."
  • Key commercial developments included revenue from the NASA LiDAR initiative using the DIRAC-3 quantum optimization machine and a transaction with a major U.S. financial institution validating QCi's quantum AI and security platforms. The Tempe, Arizona Fab 1 foundry advanced process qualification, while early-stage planning for Fab 2 began, targeting higher volume manufacturing.
  • The company increased hiring to support expansion and joined several industry consortia to raise its profile. Huang noted, "We are already in the early stage of planning for Fab 2, which we expect to begin developing over the next 3 years."
  • Christopher Roberts, CFO & General Counsel, reported, "Revenue during our third quarter totaled approximately $384,000 compared to $101,000 in the same period last year. The increase in revenue was primarily due to increases in the number of size and level of effort on research and development services contracts and custom hardware contracts."
  • Roberts added, "We ended the third quarter with cash and cash equivalents of $352 million and investments of $460 million on our balance sheet at the end of the quarter. After the end of the third quarter, we also closed on an additional $750 million financing in October."

Outlook

  • The company outlined a current 3-year roadmap focused on refining processes, scaling small batch production, and expanding team and facilities to position for industry-scale output. Management emphasized, "Over the next 3 years, our road map priority is small-scale, high-value manufacturing as we refine processes, demonstrate performance across customer applications and established supply chain and design partnerships."
  • No explicit guidance figures or analyst estimate comparisons were provided.

Financial Results

  • Quantum Computing Inc. reported revenue of $384,000 for Q3 2025 and a gross margin of 33%. Cash and cash equivalents totaled $352 million, with investments of $460 million at quarter end. Operating expenses for the quarter were $10.5 million, reflecting growth in personnel and scaling efforts.
  • The company recorded net income of $2.4 million for the quarter, or $0.01 per share, attributed to a $9.2 million gain from the mark-to-market of a derivative liability and $3.5 million in interest income. Total assets reached $898 million, and total liabilities were $20 million, down $26 million from year-end 2024.
  • Stockholders' equity rose to $878 million at the end of the quarter.

Q&A

  • Maxwell Michaelis, Lake Street Capital Markets: Asked for detail on the POET Technologies partnership and other similar pipeline opportunities. Huang responded that the collaboration leverages thin-film lithium niobate for next-generation high-speed transceiver technology, highlighting industry recognition and plans to explore additional telecom and datacom applications.
  • Michaelis inquired about broader use cases for the quantum security solution in the financial sector. Huang explained ongoing discussions with firms in wireless, internet, and aerospace sectors about integrating quantum communication technology into photonic integrated chips, targeting long-term quantum internet applications.
  • Michaelis asked about M&A strategy. Roberts said, "We are looking actively for acquisition candidates and evaluating them as they come up...we're looking at M&A as a way of doing 2 things. One is to acquire customers and revenue and product lines that can be moved forward and migrated forward with our technologies. And we're also looking to fill in some key aspects of our own technology road map."
  • Troy Jensen, Cantor Fitzgerald: Asked about remaining CapEx for Fab 1 and Fab 2. Huang stated Fab 1 is fully operational with plans for an additional $2 million in high-speed testing equipment. Fab 2 is scoped to support both QCi's needs and external demand, with a goal to produce hundreds of millions of chips per year.
  • Jensen asked for share count projections. Roberts confirmed 224 million shares outstanding for Q4, with some potential increase depending on option exercises.
  • Jensen requested stats on QPU performance and near-term opportunity focus. Huang discussed upgrades to the DIRAC-3 system, progress on a new architecture, and sales in quantum sensing, communication, AI, and foundry services.
  • John McPeake, Rosenblatt Securities, questioned quantum security pipeline growth amid advances in Shor's algorithm. Huang stressed the urgency of quantum-secured internet and QCi's compatibility with existing fiber infrastructure.
  • McPeake also asked about DIRAC-3 optimization market trends. Huang identified three commercialization hurdles: customer awareness, integration with existing systems, and lowering entry barriers through user-friendly, compatible, and affordable devices.
  • Edward Woo, Ascendiant Capital, asked about international opportunities. Huang cited sales to TU Delft in Europe, partnerships in South Korea, and users in Singapore.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts posed detailed, forward-looking questions, with a neutral to slightly positive tone, expressing interest in partnerships, pipeline expansion, and international opportunities.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone throughout, emphasizing operational progress and strategic clarity. Huang stated, "The quantum era is unfolding faster than most predicted, and our team is determined to ensure QCi remains at the center of transformation."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts appeared more focused on scaling, commercialization, and strategic partnerships, with increased confidence reflected in management's responses.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language evolved from highlighting technology integration and initial commercial wins in Q2 to a sharper focus in Q3 on scaling manufacturing, expanding foundry operations, and advancing strategic partnerships (e.g., POET Technologies).
  • Key metrics improved, with revenue rising from $61,000 in Q2 to $384,000 in Q3, and a move from a net loss of $36 million in Q2 to net income of $2.4 million in Q3, largely due to one-time gains and increased capital.
  • Operating expenses increased in line with expansion plans. The tone of management shifted from cautious optimism to greater confidence in scaling and execution.
  • Analysts' questions shifted from technical milestones and OpEx to commercialization, M&A, and global opportunities.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted that gross margin remains variable due to the small number of active contracts and custom work.
  • Roberts cautioned, "with a small number of active contracts, some of which involve custom design work, gross margin is likely to be variable from one period to the next."
  • Analyst concerns centered on the scalability of current technology, the pace of international expansion, and the pipeline for commercial quantum security solutions.
  • No formal M&A announcements, with Roberts acknowledging ongoing market volatility and uncertainty in valuations.

Final Takeaway

Quantum Computing Inc. enters the final stretch of 2025 with a strengthened financial position and a clear roadmap focused on scaling manufacturing and deepening commercial partnerships. The company reported significant capital raises, operational progress at its foundry, and early traction with major commercial and government clients. Management emphasized its readiness to transition from prototype to volume production over the next three years, supported by a growing team and expanding industry presence, positioning QCi as a first-mover in practical, scalable quantum and photonic solutions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Sidus Space outlines late 2026 LizzieSat-4 and LizzieSat-5 launches while expanding all-domain product commercialization
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sidus Space (SIDU) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Carol Craig stated that Sidus Space continued expanding its vertical integration, advanced the LizzieSat constellation, and strengthened partnerships serving commercial and defense customers. She explained the company aims to deliver "full-spectrum solutions from design and manufacturing to on-orbit operations and data services with the agility to meet evolving mission requirements."
  • Management highlighted a strategic shift toward diversified, recurring revenue models and detailed advancements in LizzieSat, describing the satellites as "precision-engineered microsatellites built to deliver mission-critical performance."
  • Craig announced significant progress on the mobile launch 2 contract, noting it expanded from an original value of approximately $4 million to over $8 million and is nearing completion, setting the stage for "improved gross margins and stronger revenue visibility as well as a reconfigured facility ready for expanded satellite and defense manufacturing."
  • The CEO emphasized the launch cadence, reporting that "in just over a year, we launched 3 Sidus-designed, Sidus-built hybrid 3D-printed satellites with onboard AI and multiple sensors at a pace that allowed us to rapidly integrate lessons learned into each successive mission."
  • Craig stated, "This quarter, we completed 2 successful capital raises with funds to be invested in commercializing all-domain product lines, expanding the LizzieSat constellation with LizzieSat-4 and LizzieSat-5, and advancing our Orlaith AI ecosystem."
  • Adarsh Parekh, CFO, said, "Momentum from 2024 in the first half of 2025 carried into the third quarter of 2025, which reflects both our transition to commercialization of dual-use, multi-domain products and the near-term financial impacts of scaling a deep-tech, space-based enterprise."

Outlook

  • Management indicated that 2 additional LizzieSat spacecrafts are in production for a planned late 2026 launch, featuring "advanced software-defined imagers and increased onboard processing capability."
  • CEO Craig emphasized the expectation of "improved gross margins and stronger revenue visibility" as the mobile launcher 2 contract completes and more resources shift to "higher-margin satellite and data programs."
  • The company expects the Fortis VPX computing suite, which is "on track for year-end validation," to contribute revenue starting in 2026.

Financial Results

  • CFO Parekh reported total revenue for the first 9 months of 2025 was approximately $2.8 million, down from $3.8 million in the same period in 2024, attributing the decline to a strategic shift away from legacy contract work toward higher-value commercial space and AI-driven solutions.
  • For the 3 months ended September 30, 2025, total revenue reached $1.3 million, a decrease from about $1.9 million in Q3 2024.
  • Cost of revenue for the quarter rose to $2.6 million, up 42% from the prior year, primarily due to increased depreciation and higher input costs.
  • Gross profit for Q3 2025 was a loss of $1.3 million compared to a profit of $38,000 in Q3 2024, with CFO Parekh stating this was "primarily due to higher depreciation from recently capitalized assets, which are essential to future revenue streams, contract mix evolution, reduced contribution from legacy services as we transition to higher margin recurring revenue models."
  • SG&A expenses for the quarter totaled $4.3 million, up from $3.2 million in Q3 2024, reflecting "strategic head count growth aligned with our move to higher-value offerings, expanded mission operations for satellite support, increased software infrastructure investment, accrued equity compensation and employee bonuses and higher depreciation expense."
  • Adjusted EBITDA loss for Q3 2025 was $4.0 million, an increase from Q3 2024. Net loss for the quarter was $6 million compared to $3.9 million in the same period of the prior year.
  • As of September 30, 2025, Sidus had $12.7 million in cash.

Q&A

  • CEO Craig responded to a question about Fortis VPX commercialization, indicating the first three products are on track for release to production in January 2026 and that "customer integrations and revenue contributions" are expected soon after.
  • When asked about the LizzieSat-3 commissioning and the impact of additional satellites, Craig explained, "The LS-3 commissioning is progressing well because there are multiple payloads and sensors along with our integration of updated and enhanced software, it isn't a quick process. But our satellites have a 5-year design life, and we're manufactured with that time line in mind. The additional satellites in production, currently LS-4 and LS-5 expand hosted payload capability, data availability and on-orbit AI throughout."
  • On the Fortis VPX and FeatherEdge customer pipeline, Craig stated, "We have active early access programs with both government and commercial customers for our proven FeatherEdge platform. And several have already begun transitioning toward multiyear hardware agreements."
  • Regarding geographic revenue mix, Craig noted "strong momentum internationally, especially among allies seeking sovereign U.S. origin multi-domain capabilities."
  • On backlog, Craig indicated it is "increasingly being driven by VPX SOSA hardware, engineering services and LizzieSat integrations."
  • Discussing the recent capital raise, Craig said it was intended to "accelerate innovation and then fund growth."
  • On alignment with DoD priorities, Craig explained, "We believe Sidus' technology road map aligns directly with that need. Our strength in autonomy, rapid deployment, ruggedized edge computing and multi-mission sensing allow us to deliver space-based nodes that are capable of operating as part of a larger adaptive defense network."
  • Regarding the lunar-capable LizzieSat, Craig stated, "There are very few U.S. companies that could provide smaller, cost-effective lunar buses, and we believe our early-mover position creates a strategic opportunity."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' questions centered on product commercialization timelines, customer adoption, and revenue mix, with a neutral to slightly positive tone, seeking clarity on execution and market positioning.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing adaptability, recurring revenue, and innovation: "I'm confident that Sidus is built to remain resilient and adaptable regardless of the external landscape." Confidence was also reflected in statements about funding, technology progress, and alignment with defense priorities.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts' tone remains largely neutral, while management's tone has grown more optimistic about commercialization, recurring revenue, and product launches.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter emphasized diversification, completion of the mobile launch 2 contract, and new capital raises, while the previous quarter focused more on the initial commercialization and the commissioning of LizzieSat-3.
  • There is a stronger emphasis this quarter on recurring revenue, defense and commercial customer expansion, and further product launches, particularly the late 2026 planned launches for LizzieSat-4 and LizzieSat-5.
  • Key metrics show revenue and profit declines compared to the previous year, with continued investment in infrastructure, personnel, and R&D.
  • Management's tone this quarter highlights increased confidence in the shift from R&D to commercialization and recurring revenue, while analysts' focus remains on execution and customer traction.
  • Strategic priorities have evolved from establishing product-market fit to accelerating commercialization and scaling production.

Risks and Concerns

  • CFO Parekh cited "ongoing global supply chain pressures impacting manufacturing operations" and higher input costs as challenges.
  • Management warned that "uncertainty around government funding and shifting federal budgets" could affect revenue timing but cited a diversified revenue model as a hedge.
  • The CFO also referenced "the near-term financial impacts of scaling a deep-tech, space-based enterprise."
  • Management indicated plans for "meaningful cost reduction activity and operating efficiencies to support long-term profitability."

Final Takeaway

Sidus Space management highlighted progress in vertical integration, rapid satellite deployment, and a strategic pivot toward recurring, diversified revenue streams. The company is preparing for the commercialization of new all-domain product lines and expects revenue contributions from the Fortis VPX suite beginning in 2026. With LizzieSat-4 and LizzieSat-5 in production for a targeted late 2026 launch and a completed capital raise supporting expansion, Sidus Space positions itself for scalable growth in commercial, defense, and civil markets, despite ongoing supply chain and budgetary uncertainties.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Modiv Industrial signals systematic asset recycling and heightened selectivity amid volatile deal market
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Modiv Industrial, Inc. (MDV) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Aaron Halfacre, President, CEO & Director, did not provide formal prepared remarks, instead stating the company had intentionally delayed its call to observe broader market reactions and noted, "I feel generally optimistic. I mean, look, I don't -- no one knows where December -- will Powell be in December. Are they done or not? But I think we all probabilistically underwrite that there's going to be a new Fed regime come May. And so -- and that regime has a high propensity to be easing." Halfacre emphasized the company’s durability and stronger balance sheet, saying, "I feel like our balance sheet is stronger. I feel like our [indiscernible] was better. I feel -- I just -- I have much more clarity now than I did even a year ago. And so I think that leads to optimism."
  • Raymond Pacini, Executive VP, CFO, Secretary & Treasurer, reported, "Yes, there was a $300,000 fee that we obtained for terminating some easement rights that are marked for property, and that's it."

Outlook

  • Management indicated a deliberate and systematic approach to asset recycling, with Halfacre explaining, "You should expect that we will be deliberate and systematic about asset recycling. If you think back, right, so we did the asset recycling the GFPR, which is a large bulk." He also stated the company’s buy box has become much tighter: "We've gotten much more selective. But that said, it feels right now at least and it's usually odd because candidly, you don't tend to see a lot at year-end."
  • The company is monitoring capital markets for signs of easing and increased deal flow, with Halfacre noting, "If we start to see real easing and we start to see some consistent trends for REITs... it's pretty cloudy. But even despite that cloudiness, I feel pretty optimistic about what I'm seeing."

Financial Results

  • A onetime $300,000 fee was recognized in other property income in the third quarter as disclosed by Pacini. There were no additional explicit financial metrics, such as revenue, AFFO, or EPS, stated in the transcript for the third quarter.
  • The company added another asset to the held-for-sale bucket, specifically the Clara property, with the aim to sell by year-end or early January. The Costco property remains held for sale, with KB Home expected to close by December 15, with the possibility of an extension to February 15.

Q&A

  • Craig Kucera, Lucid Capital Markets, LLC: Asked about onetime revenue adjustments and upcoming asset sales. Halfacre and Pacini responded by confirming the $300,000 fee was a onetime event and discussed the held-for-sale status of Clara and Costco properties.
  • Kucera: Inquired about acquisition opportunities. Halfacre observed, "We've probably looked at 4 or 5 deals in the last week," but emphasized increased selectivity and a healthier pipeline in terms of quantity, though quality remains a challenge.
  • Gaurav Mehta, Alliance Global Partners: Asked about cap rates and asset recycling's role in funding acquisitions. Halfacre reported cap rates mainly in the "7 handles" for target properties and reiterated a systematic approach to asset recycling.
  • John Massocca, B. Riley Securities: Questioned sources of future growth capital and impact of market conditions. Halfacre suggested the company is waiting for greater market clarity before accessing external growth capital, and prefers not to issue new equity or preferred unless market conditions improve.
  • Massocca: Sought insight on tenant sentiment regarding tariffs. Halfacre stated, "There's no blood in the streets and our businesses are operating. I mean most of our businesses buy U.S. and sell U.S., right?"
  • Stephen Chick: Asked about same-store rental income and property expense impacts from divestitures. Pacini said, "our overall average is 2.5% rent growth for the year just based on escalations in the leases."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious interest in deal flow, asset sales, and capital sources, with a neutral to slightly positive tone as they probed for clarity on transaction timing and capital recycling plans.
  • Management maintained an optimistic, candid, and somewhat gritty tone throughout, with Halfacre stating, "I feel pretty optimistic about what I'm seeing," but also acknowledging market volatility and the need for patience.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone shifted from frustration over inactivity to cautious optimism about recent increases in deal opportunities and a clearer strategic direction.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company has moved from a previously "boring" or stagnant quarter to a more active approach, reporting increased deal flow and heightened selectivity.
  • Strategic focus has shifted toward systematic asset recycling, with more emphasis on timing and quality of acquisitions.
  • Analysts continued to focus on asset sales, acquisition opportunities, and capital market access, but now also pressed for specifics on timing and quality of pipeline deals.
  • Management's tone has become more optimistic, with greater clarity and confidence in the durability of the balance sheet and the company’s ability to adapt.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified market volatility, the unpredictability of capital flows, and the cloudy outlook for REITs as ongoing challenges.
  • Asset recycling and transaction timing face potential headwinds from cap rate movements and holiday season slowdowns.
  • Analysts probed for risks related to tenant sentiment, tariff impacts, and the ability to complete expected property sales on schedule.

Final Takeaway

Modiv Industrial, Inc. signals a period of growing optimism and operational clarity, underscored by a disciplined, systematic approach to asset recycling and heightened selectivity in acquisitions. Management sees increased deal flow and expects continued deliberate asset rotation, while maintaining confidence in the company’s resilience and balance sheet strength despite a volatile market environment.

data.title: Nelson Peltz's Trian Fund exits U-Haul and Allstate in Q3
data.description:
Trian Fund Management exited its stake in trucks, trailers, and storage rental firm U-Haul (UHAL) and insurer Allstate (ALL) in Q3 2025. 

The fund disclosed the information in its latest 13F filing for the three months ended September 30, 2025, published on Friday.

Founded in 2005 by billionaire investor Nelson Peltz, Trian is famed for activist campaigns against companies such as Procter & Gamble (PG) and Walt Disney (DIS). According to Forbes, Peltz's real-time net worth is $1.6B.

The fund in Q3 also marginally increased its stake in fast-food chain Wendy's (WEN) by 2.2K shares.

  • Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway adds Google stake, exits D.R. Horton, among other Q3 trades
  • Carl Icahn's top Q3 moves include new stake in EchoStar, exiting Bausch Health
  • Druckenmiller's Duquesne takes in Amazon, Google, Meta, exits Microsoft, Lilly in Q3 moves
  • Seth Klarman's Baupost takes stake in Union Pacific, exits ViaSat, among Q3 trades
  • Renaissance Technologies lowers stake in Palantir, takes new position in Tesla in Q3 moves

data.title: Oil rallies as major Ukraine drone strike highlights vulnerability of Russian crude flows
data.description:
Oil futures rebounded Friday, with WTI back at the $60/bbl level after a major Ukrainian drone and missile strike on a key Russian Black Sea port revived concerns about supply disruptions.

The attack on the Novorossiysk port, which handles oil cargoes from Russia as well as oil exports from Kazakhstan, was one of the biggest on Russian oil-exporting infrastructure in recent months and reportedly caused a temporarily suspension of oil exports - amounting to the equivalent to 2.2 million bbl/day, or 2% of global supply.

Ukraine said it separately struck an oil refinery in Russia's Saratov region and a fuel storage facility in nearby Engels overnight.

"The intensity of these attacks has increased; it's much more often. Eventually, they could hit something that causes lasting disruption," UBS commodity analyst Giovanni Staunovo said in a note.

Also, Iran seized an oil tanker en route to Singapore after it passed the Strait of Hormuz. in the first major escalation of the tensions in the Middle East region since the Israel-Iran war in June.

Still, WTI futures remain down 16% YTD on growing expectations of an oversupplied market, with U.S. crude inventories rising last week in a sign of weaker demand in the world's largest consumer, and OPEC estimating that global supply outpaced demand in Q3 and forecasting a significant supply surplus.

Crude price gains continue to be limited by the fundamental backdrop, Joseph Dahrieh of Tickmill said in a note, with groups including the IEA forecasting a significant supply surplus, adding that "the market could remain under pressure despite occasional bounces driven by geopolitical headlines."

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for December delivery closed +2.4% on Friday to $60.09/bbl, and front-month Brent December crude finished +2.2% to $64.39/bbl; for the week, Nymex and Brent ended up 0.6% and 1.2%, respectively.

U.S. natural gas (NG1:COM) fell Friday but settled well above the day's lows, with the front-month Nymex December contract -1.7% to $5.5660/MMBtu after the EIA reported inventories rose by a higher than forecast 45 Bcf last week to 3,960 Bcf, or 172 Bcf above the five-year average; for the week, natgas rose for the fourth consecutive week, up 5.8%.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

Energy stocks, as represented by the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE), finished +1.8% this week.

Top 10 gainers in energy and natural resources in the past 5 days: Spruce Power (SPRU) +90.8%, Orion Energy Systems (OESX) +39.9%, FTC Solar (FTCI) +26.1%, Baytex Energy (BTE) +25.4%, Braskem (BAK) +23%, Drilling Tools International (DTI) +20.8%, Smart Sand (SND) +19.6%, Nexa Resources (NEXA) +19%, Albemarle (ALB) +18.5%, Canadian Solar (CSIQ) +17.5%.

Top 10 decliners in energy and natural resources in the past 5 days: ESS Tech (GWH) -37.8%, Nuscale Power (SMR) -26%, New Era Energy & Digital (NUAI) -25.2%, American Resources (AREC) -24.7%, Eos Energy Enterprises (EOSE) -24.4%, ProFrac Holding (ACDC) -24%, Sable Offshore (SOC) -21.5%, Atlas Energy Solutions (AESI) -19.8%, American Battery Technology (ABAT) -18.7%, Bloom Energy (BE) -17.2%.

Source: Barchart.com

data.title: SoftBank's Q3 moves include new stakes in Intel and Klarna, exiting Oracle
data.description:
SoftBank (OTCPK:SFTBY) took new stakes in Intel (INTC) and Klarna (KLAR) in Q3 2025, among other moves. 

The Japanese multinational investment company disclosed new buys in Intel (INTC), adding about 87M shares to its holdings, as well as 15.4M new shares in Klarna (KLAR), as per its 13F filing for the quarter.

Looking at exits, SoftBank (OTCPK:SFTBY) shed its positions in Oracle (ORCL) and Cipher Mining (CIFR).

Additionally, the company reduced its stake in T-Mobile (TMUS) by 29.3% to 45.2M shares and in Lemonade (LMND) by 40.2% to 3.6M shares.

SoftBank's (OTCPK:SFTBY) filing comes just a few days after its earnings report for the six months ended September 30, in which it revealed that it had unloaded its entire stake in the world's largest company, Nvidia (NVDA), for $5.83B in October. Friday's 13F filing showed that, just before that sale, the Japanese giant held 32.1M NVDA shares.

  • Tudor Investment adds Meta, Broadom, Cyberark, among top Q3 trades
  • Renaissance Technologies lowers stake in Palantir, takes new position in Tesla in Q3 moves
  • Soros Capital adds Vimeo, exits Titan America, among Q3 trades
